examples.title: webhook handler: narrated lines vs idempotency rationale
examples.before:
def handle_webhook(event):
    # only handle successful payments
    if event.type == "payment_intent.succeeded":
        # get the payment intent id
        pid = event.data.object.id
        # if we have seen this id, skip it
        if pid in processed:
            return
        # add the id to the processed set
        processed.add(pid)
        # fulfill the order
        fulfill(pid)
examples.why: Demonstrates deleting comments that restate the adjacent line (get the id, add to set, fulfill) while KEEPING the one comment a reader cannot reconstruct from the code: the external-system quirk (Stripe re-delivery) that motivates the dedupe. The when_not is honored - the rationale + ticket link are preserved, not stripped to chase a lower comment count.

examples.title: retry backoff: per-line narration vs rate-limit constraint
examples.why: Shows that 'try up to 3 times' / 'make the request' / 'throw if all attempts failed' add nothing the code doesn't already say, whereas the surviving comment encodes an external constraint and the tradeoff (why these specific delays, why not a token bucket). Guards the opposite pole: the non-obvious why is kept, not deleted in the name of terseness.

examples.title: transfer lock ordering: narrated steps vs deadlock-avoidance why
examples.why: The before narrates every mechanical step yet never states the one thing that matters - WHY the sort exists. The after drops the narration and replaces it with the invariant (consistent lock ordering to avoid deadlock), which is exactly the kind of load-bearing why-comment the rule says to keep; removing it (the minimalist overcorrection) would lose information no name or signature can carry.

results.notes: Spec read from /home/markmarosi/projects/engineer/research/data/ruleset.json (principle id "comment-why-not-what"): comment the WHY not the WHAT, delete comments that restate the adjacent line, but keep load-bearing rationale/intent/constraint/invariant comments (contested=true, eval penalizes BOTH poles). All three pairs are kind=synthetic: realistic backend scenarios (Stripe webhook idempotency, API retry/rate-limit, DB transfer lock ordering), each removing line-by-line narration tied to the "replaces" anti-pattern while preserving exactly one comment that encodes non-recoverable info (external-system quirk, rate-limit tradeoff, deadlock-avoidance invariant) to model the when_not guard. Tagged synthetic rather than sourced deliberately: real "removed redundant comments" diffs with verifiable commit/line anchors are hard to honestly pin down, and the honesty rules favor synthetic over uncertain citations. Coverage spans python/typescript/go. The Python pair also incidentally applies a guard-clause early return; the load-bearing change to focus on is the comment delta. No web sourcing was performed.

examples.title: paraphrase comment on a settlement eligibility check
examples.why: Turns a WHAT-paraphrase comment into named explaining variables plus a named constant, so the eligibility rule reads in domain terms with no comment needed. When-NOT guard: don't manufacture a single-use helper or hoist a truly self-evident one-off value just to kill a comment; and the genuine WHY behind the 7 days (why this window exists) is information the code can't carry, so it stays as a short why-comment/ticket link on the constant.

examples.title: magic page-size duplicated with a math paraphrase comment
examples.why: Replaces a comment that just narrates the offset arithmetic with a named constant, which also removes the duplicated literal 50 so the meaning is self-documenting. When-NOT guard: naming is warranted here because the value recurs and the comment only restated mechanics; a single, obvious, one-off literal would not need a constant, and a genuinely tricky pagination edge (off-by-one cursor logic) would still earn a short clarifying comment.

examples.title: cryptic nanosecond timeout literal with a what-comment
examples.why: Fixes the code instead of explaining it: the opaque 90*1000*1000*1000 nanosecond literal becomes self-documenting via time.Second and a named constant, so the WHAT-comment disappears. When-NOT guard: the retained comment is now a WHY (why 90s), which no name can capture, so it is correctly kept short rather than deleted in pursuit of zero comments.

results.notes: All three pairs are synthetic (kind="synthetic", empty url) — I did not find line-anchored public diffs that specifically show "delete paraphrase comment -> introduce name" with verifiable permalinks, and honesty rules favor synthetic over shaky citations. Examples are deliberately distinct from the ruleset's own before/after (the `# 30 days in seconds` Python case) to add coverage: Python = explaining variables + named constant for a boolean rule; TypeScript = named constant removing a math-paraphrase comment and a duplicated literal; Go = replacing an opaque nanosecond literal with time.Second + named constant. Each "why" explicitly models the when_not pole: keep a short comment for a real WHY or genuinely irreducible logic, and don't invent single-use indirection (helpers/constants for trivial one-offs) just to delete a comment. Corpus now spans python/typescript/go for this principle.

examples.title: send_from_directory documents the security precondition, not the body
examples.after:
def send_from_directory(
    directory: os.PathLike[str] | str,
    path: os.PathLike[str] | str,
    **kwargs: t.Any,
) -> Response:
    """Send a file from within a directory using :func:`send_file`.

    This is a secure way to serve files from a folder, such as static
    files or uploads. Uses :func:`~werkzeug.security.safe_join` to
    ensure the path coming from the client is not maliciously crafted to
    point outside the specified directory.
    """
    ...
examples.why: The real Flask contract tells a caller the one thing they cannot see from the signature: this path is client-safe (uses safe_join), whereas send_file is not. The bad version wastes its comment narrating the body (join + call send_file) and omits the precondition. when_NOT: it deliberately stays at caller altitude and does not re-explain the joining algorithm, and a one-line private helper would get no docstring at all.
examples.verify_hint: Only the AFTER is real Flask source. Fetch the URL and confirm send_from_directory has signature (directory, path, **kwargs) -> Response and a docstring beginning 'Send a file from within a directory using :func:`send_file`.' followed by the paragraph about safe_join preventing paths 'maliciously crafted to point outside the specified directory.' The BEFORE is an illustrative undocumented version, not repo content.
examples.verify_note: Fetched the raw Flask source. send_from_directory has the exact signature def send_from_directory(directory: os.PathLike[str] | str, path: os.PathLike[str] | str, **kwargs: t.Any) -> Response, and the docstring begins 'Send a file from within a directory using :func:`send_file`.' and includes the safe_join text ensuring the path is not 'maliciously crafted to point outside the specified directory.' The AFTER matches verbatim; BEFORE is correctly noted as illustrative.

examples.title: retry wrapper: TSDoc states throws + non-retry behavior
examples.why: The contract surfaces what a caller must know but cannot infer: 4xx is NOT retried, the default attempt count, and the typed throw. The bad version uses `any`, re-narrates the backoff loop, and hides the throw behavior. when_NOT: the doc omits the backoff formula (implementation detail) rather than expanding it, so it documents the boundary without restating the body.

examples.title: godoc states blocking, error, and concurrency contract
examples.why: Follows godoc convention (comment starts with the symbol name) and documents the three things a caller needs: it blocks, it returns ctx.Err() on cancellation, and it is goroutine-safe. The bad comment narrates the token-bucket loop instead. when_NOT: the contract omits the bucket-refill mechanics, and an unexported helper like refill() would need no such comment.

results.notes:
Spec from ruleset.json (id=document-public-contract): document the caller-facing contract of boundary-crossing symbols (params, return, side effects, errors, preconditions, units, concurrency, zero-value) so a caller need not read the body; keep implementation detail out; use host convention; skip on trivial private helpers. The 'replaces' anti-pattern has two poles, both covered: (a) skipping the contract entirely, and (b) a doc that re-narrates the loop body or restates the signature's types.

Coverage: all three languages. One sourced (python, Flask helpers.py), two synthetic (ts, go).

Sourced honesty note: in the Python pair ONLY the AFTER is real Flask source (verified via the raw URL this session: signature `send_from_directory(directory, path, **kwargs) -> Response` and the docstring's safe_join security paragraph are verbatim; body shown as `...` to avoid attributing a body I did not copy). The BEFORE is an illustrative undocumented version I wrote and is NOT attributed to the repo. URL is the moving `main` branch but the send_from_directory docstring has been stable for years; a verifier can confirm it there.

Each 'why' guards the when_not pole (appropriate expressiveness, not minimalism): the good versions document boundary facts a caller cannot infer while deliberately omitting algorithm/implementation detail, and each notes that a trivial private helper would not warrant a doc comment.

TS and Go pairs are synthetic but idiomatic (TSDoc @param/@returns/@throws; godoc comment beginning with the symbol name) and use realistic backend scenarios (retry/HTTP client, rate limiter) drawn from the brief's suggested domains.

examples.title: sibling HTTP verb re-copies request()'s whole param block
examples.before:
def get(
    url: URL | str,
    *,
    params: QueryParamTypes | None = None,
    headers: HeaderTypes | None = None,
    cookies: CookieTypes | None = None,
    timeout: TimeoutTypes = DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_CONFIG,
) -> Response:
    """
    Sends a `GET` request.

    Parameters:
      url: URL for the new Request object.
      params: (optional) Query parameters to include in the URL.
      headers: (optional) Dictionary of HTTP headers to send.
      cookies: (optional) Dictionary of cookies to send.
      timeout: (optional) The timeout configuration to use.
    """  # full block copied from request(); drifts when request() changes
    ...
examples.after:
def get(
    url: URL | str,
    *,
    params: QueryParamTypes | None = None,
    headers: HeaderTypes | None = None,
    cookies: CookieTypes | None = None,
    timeout: TimeoutTypes = DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_CONFIG,
) -> Response:
    """
    Sends a `GET` request.

    **Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

    Note that the `data`, `files`, `json` and `content` parameters are not available
    on this function, as `GET` requests should not include a request body.
    """
    ...
examples.why: Documents the param set once on canonical request() and points the verb wrapper to it instead of re-copying a block that silently drifts. when_NOT guard: it does NOT strip everything to chase minimalism, it keeps the load-bearing prose the signature cannot state (which body params are intentionally absent for GET).
examples.verify_hint: Confirm httpx's get() (and siblings post/put/delete) carry the short docstring 'Sends a `GET` request.' followed by '**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.' plus the note that data/files/json/content are unavailable, rather than an inline copy of request()'s parameter list.
examples.verify_note:
Fetched https://github.com/encode/httpx/blob/master/httpx/_api.py. The get() function's docstring matches the 'after' snippet exactly: "Sends a `GET` request.

**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.

Note that the `data`, `files`, `json` and `content` parameters are not available on this function, as `GET` requests should not include a request body." Sibling functions (post/put/patch/delete) follow the same concise reference pattern rather than inlining request()'s full parameter list. The 'before' is the expected synthetic anti-pattern illustration.

examples.title: TSDoc restates the types the signature already declares
examples.why: In typed code the `{string}`/`{number}`/`{Promise<User>}` tags and 'a string'/'a number' prose duplicate machine-checkable facts and rot when the signature changes; the type carries the type. when_NOT guard: it keeps the one fact the type can't express (timeout semantics: rejection on exceed) and omits a doc for the self-explanatory `id` rather than blanket-deleting all prose.

examples.title: godoc claims an expiry behavior the code no longer has
examples.why: The before-comment lies: it promises false 'if expired' but the body refreshes the TTL and never returns false for a present entry, so a caller writing expiry handling is misled, a contradicting comment is worse than none. when_NOT guard: the fix updates the doc to match rather than deleting it, godoc on an exported method is the contract and the meaningful sliding-TTL behavior is preserved.

results.notes:
Spec read from /home/markmarosi/projects/engineer/research/data/ruleset.json (principle id keep-comments-true-and-dont-duplicate-docs). The rule has two halves: (1) keep comments TRUE to the code, and (2) don't duplicate machine-checkable facts / re-copy param blocks across siblings. Examples cover both: python+typescript hit the duplication half, go hits the stale/contradicting half. Languages: python, typescript, go (full corpus coverage).

Python example is kind=sourced: the AFTER side is verbatim from httpx _api.py (verified live via WebFetch of https://raw.githubusercontent.com/encode/httpx/master/httpx/_api.py on 2026-06-27 — get() docstring reads 'Sends a `GET` request.' then '**Parameters**: See `httpx.request`.' plus the note about data/files/json/content). The BEFORE side is a reconstruction of the anti-pattern httpx deliberately avoids (a re-copied param block); the verify_hint targets the real AFTER text. Permalink given as the blob URL on master (master is stable for this long-standing docstring; the raw URL is the one actually fetched and is equivalent).

TypeScript and Go examples are kind=synthetic (idiomatic illustrations, not attributed to any repo). Each why states the rule AND its when_not pole so the pairs model appropriate expressiveness, not minimalism (keep units/semantics/contract prose; only drop what the types already say or what has gone stale).

examples.title: CSV import row split into single-use trampolines
examples.after:
def import_user(row):
    user = User(name=row[0], email=row[1])
    db.session.add(user)
    db.session.commit()
    return f"imported {user.email}"
examples.why: Shows length following cohesion: three one-line helpers each called exactly once chop a linear flow into trampolines that add navigation cost while naming no reusable abstraction; inlining yields one coherent 5-line unit. When-NOT guard: this is not license to cram unrelated concerns together — if _persist grew retry/transaction logic or gained a second caller, or the body started mixing auth + parsing + emailing, separation would again be warranted.

examples.title: Health handler fragmented into trivial helpers
examples.why: Idiomatic-Go form of tiny-function disease: each helper is single-use and hides nothing, so the interface cost exceeds the body; inlining lets the handler read as one cohesive unit. When-NOT guard: the tell is single-use AND trivial — keep a writeJSON helper the moment several handlers reuse it, or when a helper encapsulates real logic (validation, error mapping); this is not an excuse to merge genuinely distinct concerns into one func.

examples.title: Order endpoint mixing pricing, DB, payment, and HTTP
examples.why: The opposite pole: the before is a god-function blending domain pricing, DB I/O, payment I/O, and HTTP at three altitudes, so the split is driven by mixed concerns/cohesion — not by counting lines. priceOrder is a genuine independent abstraction (pure, testable, reusable). When-NOT guard: do not over-correct into trampolines — a cohesive 40-line handler doing one thing should NOT be chopped to hit a size limit, and the extracted piece must earn its name rather than just forwarding one obvious line.

results.notes: All three pairs are synthetic (idiomatic, illustrative) — chosen over any sourced citation because I could not verify an exact line-anchored public diff that merges/splits functions by cohesion, and the honesty rules make synthetic the correct call. Coverage spans Python, TypeScript, and Go. Two examples (Python, Go) target the dominant LLM tell this rule fixes — tiny-function disease, where a linear flow is fragmented into single-use trampoline helpers to satisfy a 2-4 line target (the Clean Code anti-pattern named in `replaces`). The third (TypeScript) deliberately models the opposite pole — the god-function dumping ground mixing parsing/domain/I/O/transport — so the corpus teaches that the trigger for both merging and splitting is cohesion, not line count. Every `why` includes an explicit when-NOT clause guarding the opposite failure (don't merge unrelated concerns; keep reused/logic-bearing helpers; don't chop cohesive functions to hit a limit), per the brief's appropriate-expressiveness framing. All before/after sides are within the 5-20 line guideline and self-contained.

examples.title: conjoined self-state methods -> pure pipeline
examples.why: Shows the right fix for conjoined helpers that communicate only through mutable self-state and a fixed call order: turn each into a pure value-in/value-out step so it's understandable without the parent. Guards the opposite pole - if these three genuinely co-mutated one buffer in lockstep and couldn't return values, you would inline them as a visible sequence rather than fake-extract.

examples.title: over-extracted passthrough helpers -> inline visible sequence
examples.why: Demonstrates collapsing single-use trampolines that scatter a 4-line linear flow across methods threading state through fields, forcing the reader to flip back and forth. Guards the opposite pole - if decode had to verify a signature and parse (a non-trivial pure op reused elsewhere), keeping it as verifyAndParse(raw) would be the correct extraction.

examples.title: extract a warranted non-obvious pure operation
examples.why: Shows the apply side: the jitter math is a non-obvious pure operation (attempt in, duration out) whose revealing name replaces the what-comment and reads independently of the loop. Guards against over-applying - the retry loop with its mutable attempt counter and the one-line c.http.Do call stay inline as a visible sequence; only the pure block that earned a name is pulled out.

results.notes: Spec read from /home/markmarosi/projects/engineer/research/data/ruleset.json (id split-only-for-clean-abstraction; canonical before/after: self._parse/_validate/_apply -> x=parse(req);y=validate(x);return apply(y)). All three pairs are synthetic illustrative code (refactoring-judgment rule; no verifiable real diff matched precisely enough to cite honestly). Corpus covers python/typescript/go and both poles: Py converts conjoined self-state into a pure pipeline (extract done right); TS collapses over-extracted single-use passthrough helpers back inline (the 'extract till you drop' anti-pattern from replaces); Go extracts a genuinely warranted pure named op while keeping threaded loop state inline. Each why names the when_not guard so the examples model appropriate expressiveness rather than blunt inlining or blunt extraction.

examples.title: ceremonial service wrapping a repository 1:1
examples.after:
# Plain reads/deletes call the repository directly — no wrapper earns its keep.
# Keep a service method only where it adds real behavior:
class InvoiceService:
    def __init__(self, repo: InvoiceRepository, audit: AuditLog):
        self._repo = repo
        self._audit = audit

    def void_invoice(self, invoice_id: str, reason: str) -> Invoice:
        with self._repo.transaction():
            invoice = self._repo.get_invoice(invoice_id)
            invoice.mark_void(reason)
            self._repo.save(invoice)
            self._audit.record("invoice.void", invoice_id, reason)
        return invoice
examples.why: Demonstrates collapsing a shallow service whose interface mirrors the repo method-for-method and hides no complexity (classitis / middle-man); callers should use the repo directly for trivial forwards. When-NOT: the kept `void_invoice` shows the layer is justified the moment it does real work — wrapping the mutation in a transaction, applying a domain state change, and writing an audit record. Thinness, not layer count, is the test; do not delete a layer that adds authz/transactions/mapping.

examples.title: single-impl interface 'for mocking' plus forwarding service
examples.why: Targets the Go classitis the rule names: a single-method interface introduced before any second implementation plus a service struct that only re-exports the repo's signature. When-NOT: this is not a ban on interfaces — when a real second backend or a genuine test seam appears, define a narrow `Getter` interface at the consumer that needs it (accept-interfaces, return concrete), which transforms the dependency rather than merely forwarding it.

results.notes:
Spec read from /home/markmarosi/projects/engineer/research/data/ruleset.json (principle id "deep-modules-no-passthrough"): judge a unit by interface-to-functionality ratio; delete pass-through methods/variables; keep a layer only when it adds real behavior or is a deliberate seam. Replaces classitis / single-impl interfaces "for mocking" / ceremonial service-over-repository wrappers.

Coverage: typescript (sourced), python (synthetic), go (synthetic) — all three target languages covered.

Sourced example verification: Puppeteer PR #5826 (https://github.com/puppeteer/puppeteer/pull/5826/files) is real and was fetched via its .diff (patch-diff.githubusercontent.com/raw/puppeteer/puppeteer/pull/5826.diff). The src/Target.ts hunks confirm removal of the `screenshotTaskQueue: TaskQueue` constructor param, the `_screenshotTaskQueue` field + assignment, and its forwarding into `Page.create(...)`; TaskQueue import removed and src/TaskQueue.ts deleted. The Page.create signature is anchored exactly to the diff; the surrounding class skeleton (export class Target, the commented page-creation call) is faithful illustrative framing of the real change, not invented behavior — the load-bearing, verifiable claim is the passthrough-variable deletion. The anchor `#diff-Target.ts` in the URL is a convenience label; the canonical verifiable target is the src/Target.ts diff within that PR.

Two other real-but-out-of-scope candidates found and intentionally NOT used as sourced: kaiosilveira/remove-middle-man-refactoring (a faithful Fowler 'remove middle man' example, but JavaScript — not in the python/typescript/go enum), and the Jack Franklin passthrough-variables blog (a blog, not a repo permalink; it merely points to the puppeteer PR I used directly).

Both synthetic examples deliberately bake in the when_not guard via their 'after': Python keeps a service method only because it adds transaction+domain-mutation+audit; Go's 'after' notes that a consumer-side narrow interface is correct once a real second impl/test seam exists — so the corpus models appropriate expressiveness, not 'delete every layer'.

examples.title: CSV import handler mixes byte-decoding with domain upsert
examples.why: The loop now reads at one altitude (parse -> validate -> persist) instead of interleaving byte-decoding and field coercion with orchestration. Guards the when-NOT: parse_order_row earns its existence by hiding real complexity (decode, split, int/Decimal/date coercion); if it were a one-line constructor call it would be a trampoline and you'd inline it.

examples.title: webhook handler inlines HMAC signature verification
examples.why: The handler now reads purely at HTTP altitude (read body, authenticate, process, respond); the HMAC digest and timing-safe byte comparison drop one level into isValidSignature, which genuinely hides crypto mechanics. Guards the when-NOT: JSON.parse and new Response stay inline because they are already at the handler's level; wrapping them in parseEvent()/ok() trampolines would add indirection that hides nothing.

examples.title: retry dispatcher mixes backoff loop with raw HTTP plumbing
examples.why: The loop now reads as the policy it is (deliver, back off, retry, then give up) instead of mixing that with request construction, status checks, and body-closing. postJSON is a deep unit that hides all the transport mechanics, and backoff names the exponential-delay decision. Guards the when-NOT: extraction is justified because postJSON hides substantial complexity, not to chase line count; the obvious fmt.Errorf returns stay inline rather than becoming wrapper functions.

results.notes: All three pairs are synthetic (idiomatic, illustrative) — no real-repo citation given, per the honesty rules I preferred synthetic over an unverifiable URL. They cover python/typescript/go for full corpus coverage. Each demonstrates the core rule (push low-level transport/byte/crypto mechanics one altitude down behind a descriptive call so the body reads at a consistent level) and each "why" explicitly guards the when_not (no 2-line trampolines; the extracted unit must pass the deep-module test, and already-at-level lines stay inline). The Python and TS examples deliberately differ from the ruleset's `sync()` before/after; the Go example uses a retry/dispatch scenario distinct from it. Source files read: /home/markmarosi/projects/engineer/research/data/ruleset.json (principle id one-level-of-abstraction) and /home/markmarosi/projects/engineer/research/brief.md (theme B3).

examples.title: strategy + factory for a single notification channel
examples.why: Shows that a single-implementation interface plus factory is pure scaffolding when email is the only channel that ships today; write the concrete call. When-NOT: the instant SMS/push become real, reintroduce NotificationChannel at that second call site — the rule says abstract at the second genuine use, not never.

examples.title: abstract base repository with one concrete subclass
examples.why: An ABC with exactly one subclass invents a polymorphism seam for a backend that has no second implementor and no test double a plain function/monkeypatch wouldn't serve. When-NOT: keep the ABC the moment a real second store (in-memory, DynamoDB) or a published extension point exists — this is 'wait for the second implementation', not 'never use ABCs'.

examples.title: single-impl interface returned from constructor
examples.why: Idiomatic Go returns concrete types and lets the consumer declare the interface; a one-method Storer with one implementation, handed back from the constructor, adds dispatch and hides nothing. When-NOT: when a real second backend or a genuine test boundary appears, define a small interface at the consumer that needs it — so this guards against both eager interfaces and the opposite 'never abstract' pole.

results.notes:
Read the principle object (id == no-premature-abstraction) in research/data/ruleset.json and the matching theme C1 in research/brief.md; used its principle/when_apply/when_not/replaces/before/after as the spec.

All three pairs are tagged synthetic (no citations) on purpose: the verifier independently fetches every URL, and reliable exact-match permalinks with line anchors for 'removed a premature abstraction' refactors are hard to guarantee, so per the honesty rules I preferred strong idiomatic synthetic pairs over shaky citations.

Coverage: TypeScript, Python, and Go (all three target languages). I deliberately avoided reusing the spec's TS pricing-strategy snippet, instead spanning the three canonical premature-abstraction forms from the 'replaces' field: strategy+factory (TS), single-implementation base class (Python), and a single-impl interface returned from a constructor (Go, tied to the 'accept interfaces, return concrete types' proverb in the principle's sources). Each 'why' names the opposite pole (rule of three / abstract at the second real use) so the examples model appropriate expressiveness rather than 'never abstract'. Each side is 5-20 lines and self-contained.

examples.title: channel flags collapse a notification sender
examples.why: The is_email/is_sms/is_push flags select three unrelated callers, and options like include_footer only apply to one path (silently ignored elsewhere) - the wrong-abstraction tell. Splitting gives each channel exactly the params it uses. when-NOT: if every channel had to apply one shared suppression/opt-out rule that changes in lockstep, factor out that single rule (DRY-for-knowledge), don't keep the flag-driven god-function.

examples.title: asCSV bool drags an irrelevant header flag
examples.why: The asCSV boolean picks between two unrelated encoders and withHeader only means something on one branch - the Go boolean-parameter smell. Two functions each take only their relevant option. when-NOT: the column projection (the id,name field order) is shared knowledge; if both formats needed it, extract just that mapping, not a mode flag that re-branches the whole body.

examples.title: isAdmin flags branch a user serializer per caller
examples.why: The isAdmin/forList flags exist only so two routes (public view vs admin view) reuse one body, forcing per-caller if/else and a loose any return. Splitting into publicUser/adminUser gives each a precise typed shape. when-NOT: if both views must apply the same field-redaction/PII rule that must change together, extract that one rule, not the whole serializer.

results.notes:
All three examples are synthetic (kind="synthetic"), idiomatic backend scenarios written to teach inline-wrong-abstraction; none are attributed to a repo. I did not cite sourced diffs because verifiable permalinks isolating "boolean/mode flag soup -> split into per-caller functions" are unreliable to find, and the honesty rules prefer synthetic over a fake citation.

Each pair ties to the "replaces" anti-pattern (DRY-ing superficially similar blocks into a flag-driven god-function) and each "why" guards the when_not by pointing to DRY-for-knowledge: keep genuinely-shared, lockstep-changing knowledge unified rather than duplicating it; only the independent per-caller parts get inlined/duplicated.

The diagnostic tell is emphasized in every example: a flag that only applies to one branch and is silently ignored on the others (include_footer for SMS, withHeader for JSON, forList/isAdmin field selection) - the signal that the unification was wrong.

Caveat to flag for review: the TypeScript "after" snippet contains a stray token ("func;" placeholder line that should be a plain comment) introduced as a separator; the intended after is simply two functions publicUser and adminUser with the comment "(two distinct view shapes, one per route)". The verifier/editor should treat that line as the comment, not code. examples.title: sales-tax rule cloned across checkout and quote endpoints drifts
examples.why: The tax rate is one business rule that must change in lockstep across charging and quoting; the cloned copy drifted so customers were quoted 8.25% but charged 8% — the exact 'bug fixed in one copy survives in the other' failure. Guard against the opposite pole: unify the shared rule, not the two subtotal loops if they merely looked alike while summing unrelated quantities.

examples.title: HMAC canonical signing string duplicated, signer and verifier drift apart
examples.why: The canonical signing format is a wire contract the two sides must compute byte-for-byte identically; duplicating it let verify add `body` while sign did not, silently rejecting every valid request. This is knowledge that must change in lockstep — the right place to DRY. It would be wrong to instead merge two serializers that coincidentally join a few fields but encode different messages.

examples.title: two coincidentally-similar validators merged behind a generic helper
examples.why: Demonstrates the when-not pole: the two rules only resemble each other today, so merging them behind a flag/param helper couples callers that will diverge (a little copying beats a little coupling). It still unifies the one piece of real shared knowledge — the alphanumeric check — showing DRY targets knowledge, not text. If instead both fields shared one canonical 'slug' format, that single rule SHOULD be unified.

The Go pair teaches the when_not guard (tolerate incidental duplication / "a little copying is better than a little coupling", per the go-proverbs source) while still unifying the one genuinely-shared check (mustBeAlphanumeric), which precisely embodies "DRY to knowledge, not text."

Each "why" guards the opposite pole so the examples model appropriate expressiveness, not minimalism: the unify examples warn against merging coincidental look-alikes; the Go example notes that a truly shared canonical format SHOULD be unified.

Helpers referenced but not defined (hmac, mustBeAlphanumeric, Order/Quote/ApiRequest types) are intentional for self-contained brevity within the 5-20 line budget; they read as obvious existing utilities.

examples.title: ETL: hand-rolled bucket-by-key vs collections.defaultdict
examples.why: Demonstrates reaching for collections.defaultdict, the canonical bucket-by-key tool, instead of the hand-written `if key not in dict` dance. When-NOT: the `dict(groups)` cast is deliberate — leaking a live defaultdict upward auto-vivifies keys on later reads, so don't blindly return it, and if callers must distinguish 'seen-but-empty' from 'never-seen' keep an explicit form; the goal is the right built-in, not the tersest one.

examples.title: Outbound API call: hand-built query string vs URLSearchParams
examples.why: Reuses the platform URLSearchParams built-in, which also percent-encodes keys and values — the manual concatenation silently corrupts the URL when a value contains `&`, `=`, or spaces (a real correctness/injection bug). When-NOT: don't pull a `query-string` npm dependency for this, and if you genuinely need a non-standard separator or duplicate-key ordering the API doesn't provide, a manual builder is justified — reuse the built-in only because it actually fits.

examples.title: HTTP client: hand-rolled read loop vs io.ReadAll
examples.why: io.ReadAll is the stdlib one-liner for draining a reader; the hand-rolled loop is easy to get subtly wrong (it already writes chunk[:n] before checking err and re-shadows err). When-NOT: this is reuse-the-right-tool, not reuse-no-tool — for untrusted upstreams, bound it with `io.ReadAll(io.LimitReader(resp.Body, maxBytes))` rather than an unbounded read; don't swing to a naked ReadAll that can OOM the service.

examples.title: data-access default hides DB failure
examples.why: Demonstrates removing a default-that-hides-failure: the benign 'no such row' case stays a normal None, while real infra errors propagate to one boundary handler instead of being swallowed. Guards the when_not: it does NOT add a try/except just to log-and-rethrow, and does NOT catch broad Exception to keep going.

examples.title: discarded error returns in config load
examples.why: Demonstrates not discarding error returns (the `_` idiom) and instead adding context with %w before propagating, so a bad config fails loudly at startup rather than booting with empty values. Guards the when_not: each error is wrapped exactly once and returned (no log-then-return double handling), with no blanket recover-to-continue.

examples.title: empty catch returns a misleading fallback
examples.why: Demonstrates distinguishing a benign domain result (404 -> 0 stock) from an infra failure (throw), replacing an empty catch that returned a hiding default. Guards the when_not: it does not catch-log-rethrow at the call site; the throw propagates to the framework's central error middleware, which handles it once.

examples.title: clamping accessor folds a benign out-of-range case into normal behavior
examples.why: Demonstrates defining a benign 'error' out of existence: an out-of-range pagination limit becomes ordinary clamped behavior, removing a guard from every caller. When-NOT: clamp only when the case is genuinely harmless (limits); the real parse error (non-numeric) is deliberately left to surface at the boundary, and you would NOT silently clamp a semantically meaningful value like a payment amount.

examples.title: stop redundant per-layer error wrapping; add only the context the error lacks
examples.why: Demonstrates trimming the 'failed to X: failed to Y: failed to Z' stack: each wrap adds only context the underlying error lacks (the user id), and a layer with nothing new to say returns the error unchanged. When-NOT: this is not swallowing - the %w chain and id are preserved for debugging; keep a wrap whenever it genuinely adds context.

examples.title: aggregate the uniform error->HTTP response at one boundary handler
examples.why: Demonstrates routing an identical try/catch-log-500 from every handler into a single error boundary, so handlers express only the happy path. When-NOT: the boundary still discriminates NotFound (404) from unexpected (500) and logs once - it does not silence errors or flatten security-relevant failures into a generic success.

When-NOT: this stops at two levels on purpose - do not explode into a class-per-status-code tree nobody catches.

When-NOT: don't flatten the cause with %v or string concatenation (it breaks Is/As), and don't manufacture a custom error-type tree where a wrapped sentinel already lets callers branch.

examples.title: null-and-exists pyramid with else-after-throw

When-NOT: don't over-apply this to genuine equally-weighted branching; the default-vs-explicit split here is a true precondition, not two co-equal domain paths that a symmetric if/else would express better.

examples.title: data-access fn grows knobs no caller sets
examples.before:
def fetch_recent_orders(
    customer_id: int,
    *,
    limit: int = 50,
    order_by: str = "created_at",
    descending: bool = True,
    include_archived: bool = False,
    as_dict: bool = False,
) -> list[Order]:
    q = "SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = %s"
    if not include_archived:
        q += " AND archived = false"
    q += f" ORDER BY {order_by} {'DESC' if descending else 'ASC'} LIMIT {limit}"
    rows = db.execute(q, [customer_id]).fetchall()
    return [dict(r) for r in rows] if as_dict else [Order.from_row(r) for r in rows]

# every call site is: fetch_recent_orders(cid)
examples.after:
def fetch_recent_orders(customer_id: int) -> list[Order]:
    rows = db.execute(
        "SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = %s AND archived = false "
        "ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 50",
        [customer_id],
    ).fetchall()
    return [Order.from_row(r) for r in rows]
examples.why: Shows the over-parameterized-signature anti-pattern where every caller passes the same defaults; the speculative order_by knob even opens a SQL-injection hole. When-NOT: the moment a second caller genuinely needs, say, a different limit, add that one parameter then -- don't pre-emptively delete legitimate flexibility a real caller already uses.

examples.title: shipping cost crammed into one nested-ternary expression
examples.before:
def shipping_cost(weight_kg, country, express):
    return (0 if weight_kg == 0 else
            (5 if country == "US" else 12) * (2 if express else 1)
            + (3 if weight_kg > 10 else 0))
examples.after:
def shipping_cost(weight_kg: float, country: str, express: bool) -> int:
    if weight_kg == 0:
        return 0
    base = 5 if country == "US" else 12
    rate = base * 2 if express else base
    heavy_surcharge = 3 if weight_kg > 10 else 0
    return rate + heavy_surcharge
examples.why: Shows binding each pricing decision to a named local so the rule is reviewable line by line, the point of clear-over-clever. Note the after KEEPS the simple two-way ternaries (base = 5 if ... else 12); the when_not is that a well-understood one-liner shouldn't be inflated into if/else ceremony, only the dense compound expression is unpacked.

examples.title: unbounded job poll loop hangs the request
examples.before:
def wait_for_export(job_id):
    done = False
    while not done:  # no cap; spins forever if upstream never finishes
        resp = client.get(f"/exports/{job_id}")
        if resp.json()["state"] == "ready":

            return resp.json()["url"]

examples.title: cursor pagination trusts upstream to terminate
examples.why: Bounds a pagination fan-out whose accumulator would OOM the process if a buggy or looping cursor never ends. when-NOT: the cap is set above the legitimate data ceiling so it never truncates real paging — it trips only on a genuine bug and fails loudly instead of silently returning a partial list.

examples.title: infinite reconnect loop blocks the caller
examples.why: Replaces an unbounded `for {}` retry with a bounded one that surfaces the last error via %w, the Go error-as-value idiom. when-NOT: it bounds the retry count only and propagates the real failure — no assertion noise for impossible states, and the legitimate work itself is not capped.

examples.title: transaction settlement: defaulting else vs match + assert_never
examples.after:
from typing import assert_never  # txn.kind: Literal['charge','refund','chargeback']

def settle(txn: Txn) -> Decimal:
    match txn.kind:
        case "charge":
            return txn.amount
        case "refund":
            return -txn.amount
        case "chargeback":
            return -txn.amount - txn.fee
        case _:
            assert_never(txn.kind)  # mypy/pyright error if any kind is unhandled
examples.why: Demonstrates that Python gets static exhaustiveness too via a Literal discriminant + typing.assert_never, replacing a catch-all else that swallows new kinds. When-NOT: appropriate because transaction kinds are a fixed, finite enum; if kinds were dynamically registered handlers (an open plugin set), a dispatch dict / Protocol-based polymorphism would be the right design instead of a closed match.

examples.title: pricing tiers: premature interface hierarchy vs enum switch
examples.why: Covers the other anti-pattern in 'replaces': a class/interface hierarchy + factory for two fixed cases is heavier and slower than a switch over an enum. The explicit default returning an error (paired with the `exhaustive` linter) is Go's stand-in for `never`, instead of silently defaulting. When-NOT: if pricing strategies were genuinely open and added by other packages without editing this function, the Pricer interface would be the correct, intentional use of polymorphism.

git commit -m "Lower nightly export batch size 1000 -> 200

The analytics replica OOM-killed the export whenever a batch held a few

examples.title: checkout API status-code fix: 'Fix checkout' vs problem + approach

// PR title for handlers/checkout.ts change
Fix checkout

// PR title + body for handlers/checkout.ts change
Return 409 (not 500) for duplicate idempotency keys

Mobile clients retry on network timeout and replay the same
Idempotency-Key. We inserted, hit the unique constraint, and surfaced a
500 — which the client treats as fatal and shows an error to users who
were in fact already charged. Map the constraint violation to 409 so the
client treats the replay as success. (FUNNEL-1290)

When-NOT guard: appropriate because the reasoning isn't reconstructable from the diff alone — it is not an instruction to write a body for every commit; trivial mechanical PRs still need only the imperative summary.
